title: Nepali Chicken Curry

ingredients.name: chicken, curry cut with bone
visual_checks: Bone-in pieces are traditional; they enrich the gravy with body.
ingredients.notes: Bone-in pieces are traditional; they enrich the gravy with body.

subs.name: 600 g bone-in mutton pieces — increase simmering time to 35 minutes or pressure-cook for 5 whistles.

ingredients.name: red chilli powder
visual_checks: Adjust down to 1 tsp for a moderately hot curry.
ingredients.notes: Adjust down to 1 tsp for a moderately hot curry.

ingredients.name: coriander powder
visual_checks: A larger quantity than most Indian curries; this is characteristic of Nepali chicken.
ingredients.notes: A larger quantity than most Indian curries; this is characteristic of Nepali chicken.

subs.name: 1 tbsp store-bought meat masala + 0.5 tbsp coriander powder if coriander powder alone is not available.

ingredients.name: timur or black pepper, coarsely ground
visual_checks: Timur (Szechuan/Sichuan pepper) is the authentic Nepali spice — it has a citrusy, numbing heat. Substitute with black pepper plus a tiny pinch of citrus zest.
ingredients.notes: Timur (Szechuan/Sichuan pepper) is the authentic Nepali spice — it has a citrusy, numbing heat. Substitute with black pepper plus a tiny pinch of citrus zest.

subs.name: A blend of ½ tsp Szechuan pepper (available at Chinese grocery stores) and ½ tsp black pepper comes closest to timur's citrusy, numbing quality.

ingredients.name: garam masala
visual_checks: Added at the end as a finishing touch.
ingredients.notes: Added at the end as a finishing touch.

ingredients.name: cooking oil
visual_checks: Mustard oil is traditional in the hills but any neutral oil works.
ingredients.notes: Mustard oil is traditional in the hills but any neutral oil works.

steps.text: Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed kadai or pot over medium-high heat. Add the cumin seeds and let them crackle for 20 seconds. Add the finely chopped onion and fry, stirring frequently, for 12 minutes until deep golden-brown and the volume has reduced by half.

Add the ginger-garlic paste and stir vigorously for 2 minutes until the raw smell has gone and the paste has darkened slightly. Add the turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder. Stir for 1 minute, adding a splash of water if the masala looks dry.

Add the finely chopped tomatoes. Cook on medium heat, mashing the tomato pieces down, for 5 minutes until the tomatoes have fully broken down, the masala has come together, and you can see oil beginning to separate around the edges. This is the signal the base is ready.

Add the chicken pieces, increase heat to high, and stir to coat every piece in the masala. Fry for 6 minutes, turning occasionally, until the chicken has lightly browned on the outside and the raw pink colour has disappeared.

Add 200 ml water and the coarsely ground timur or black pepper. Stir, bring to a boil, then reduce heat to medium-low, cover, and simmer for 15 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the gravy has thickened. Taste and adjust salt.
Sprinkle garam masala, stir, and cook uncovered for 2 more minutes so the finishing spice blooms into the sauce. Garnish with fresh coriander leaves and serve hot with steamed rice or chapati.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: black-pepper → A blend of ½ tsp Szechuan pepper (available at Chinese grocery stores) and ½ tsp black pepper comes closest to timur's citrusy, numbing quality.; chicken → 600 g bone-in mutton pieces — increase simmering time to 35 minutes or pressure-cook for 5 whistles.; coriander-seeds → 1 tbsp store-bought meat masala + 0.5 tbsp coriander powder if coriander powder alone is not available.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook identically to a North Indian chicken curry — kadai and pressure cooker both work. The main distinction is the absence of complex masalas; keep the spice list as written rather than adding garam masala early. Mustard oil (optional here) is sold across Indian markets and gives a more authentic hill-country aroma. Timur can be sourced online or from Himalayan food stores.
